title: Cardiology
flags: Adrenaline doses (you tagged this '[review]'): 'Cardiac arrest: 10mL 10,000 IV or 1mL of 1:1000 IV // 1mg 1:10,000' reads inconsistently in the source — verify the exact concentration/volume before relying on it (this is clinically important).
Digoxin toxicity list includes 'hypokalemia' and 'hypernatremia' — verify; hypokalaemia predisposes to toxicity. Preserved as written.
Sodium nitroprusside: 'is a venous and material smooth muscle' appears truncated/typo ('material' likely 'arterial').
New-onset AF: 'cardiovert if 48hrs' / 'cardiover t if 48hrs' is ambiguous — verify the <48h window. Preserved as written.
INR >9 with bleeding: 'Oral Bit K' is a typo (Vitamin K) and the >8 vs >9 thresholds should be verified against current guidance.
'Gallium is used in nuclear medicine for valium scans (tumor activity)' — 'valium' appears to be a typo; verify.
'PSECT studies' and 'Technetium 99mtC' contain typos (likely SPECT / 99mTc). Preserved as written.
'Jerrell and Lange Nielsen syndrome' — spelling (Jervell and Lange-Nielsen). Preserved as written.
Warfarin 'Target INT for any VTE remains 2-3' — 'INT' is a typo for INR.
Furosemide MOA: 'ascending limb of loop of hence' — 'hence' is a typo for Henle. Preserved as written.

Numerous minor spelling typos were preserved verbatim rather than corrected (e.g. Diltizaem, Virdians/Sanguines, Abxicimab, Sildanfil, symptorter, downscoping, radiofrequneyc, hotter monitor) — correcting could risk changing meaning.

notes.body:
Calcification of aortic valve:
- Ejection systolic murmur radiating to the carotid
- Quieter with left ventricular systolic dysfunction (also with Valsalva)
- Fx: narrow pulse pressure, **slow rising [4x wrong]**, delayed ESM, **soft S2**
- **Severe stenosis: soft second heart sound / displaced apex beat / S4 (heart sound before S1) [S4 3x wrong]**
- **S4 coincides with the P waves on an ECG [2x wrong]**

MC cause:
- Young (<65): bicuspid aortic valve
- Older (>65): calcification

Management — **Replacement if:**
- **symptomatic (exertional dyspnea)**
- **peak gradient >40**
- **valve area of <1**

- Associated with **rheumatic fever, syphilis**, **Marfan's syndrome, ankylosing spondylitis**, bicuspid aortic valve and post-rheumatic disease
- **Murmur: early diastolic murmur [4x wrong]**
- Abrupt distention and collapse of the carotid arteries
- Visualisation of capillary pulsations with light compression applied to the fingernail bed

Pulmonary stenosis: ejection systolic, louder on inspiration.

Mitral stenosis:
- **Mid diastolic rumbling murmur** best heard at the apex
- Sx: haemoptysis, dyspnoea; severity indicated by a longer murmur
- Tapping apex beat and parasternal heave
- Management: **Percutaneous mitral commissurotomy if severe (secondary to rheumatic) [2x wrong]**

Mitral regurgitation:
- Pan-systolic murmur louder during expiration; MR → mitral clip insertion
- Heavily calcified valve / significant MR / anatomical consideration → MV replacement

MVP associated with: osteogenesis imperfecta, pseudoxanthoma, Turner's and Marfan's.

Tricuspid regurgitation:
- **Systolic murmur louder during inspiration [2x wrong]**
- **Left parasternal heave is a feature [3x wrong]**

PDA: associated with a **collapsing pulse** (left-to-right shunt); Ibuprofen or indomethacin if still present after 1 week of birth; apex beat due to LV overload; wide pulse pressure; left subclavicular thrill.

VSD: associated with right heart failure, aortic regurgitation, Eisenmenger's complex, and infective endocarditis.

- Antibiotic prophylaxis to prevent infective endocarditis is NOT recommended for dental procedures — general oral hygiene advice is given instead
- Rheumatic heart fever histology: Aschoff bodies & Anitschkow cells

Valve replacements:
- Young / cardiomyopathy / endocarditis / rheumatic fever → mechanical valve
- Childbearing woman / bleeding RF → prosthetic (bioprosthetic) valve

- Cyanotic → **TGA MC at birth**, but overall **Fallot's most common**
- Acyanotic → VSD most common

Tetralogy of Fallot:
- Right ventricular outflow tract obstruction
- Right-to-left shunt
- Right ventricular hypertrophy
- Ejection systolic murmur
- Over-riding aorta

Transposition of great vessels: failure of the aorticopulmonary septum to spiral.
Blue toe syndrome: cholesterol embolisation.

Aetiology: viral, TB, uraemia; post-MI; radiotherapy; CT diseases (SLE, RA); hypothyroidism; lung and breast Ca; trauma.

ECG: global widespread saddle-shaped ST elevations, **PR depressions**.

Management:
- NSAIDs and Colchicine first-line for 1-2 weeks
- Inpatient only if high fever or elevated troponin
- Avoid strenuous activity
- Haemodialysis if uraemic pericarditis
- Any reading above 140/90: offer ABPM

CHA2DS2-VASc:
- C: congestive heart failure
- H: hypertension
- A2: age 75 or above (2), or age 65-74 (1)
- D: DM
- S2: prior stroke, TIA, or thromboembolism (2)
- V: vascular disease
- S: sex female

Anticoagulation:
- 0: no treatment
- 1: males consider anticoagulation, female no treatment
- 2: offer anticoagulation (DOACs) — apixaban, dabigatran

Rate control: beta-blockers; CCBs (if contraindication e.g. asthma); Digoxin.

Anticoagulation duration:
- Lifelong warfarin → 2 or more episodes
- **4 weeks warfarin and review → 1 episode**
- Idarucizumab is the reversal agent for dabigatran.

Dilated:
- MC idiopathic, or coxsackie, HTN, haemochromatosis, alcohol
- Can be inherited (DMD); nutritional (thiamine deficiency) or selenium deficiency (Keshan disease)
- Balloon appearance of the heart on CXR
- Managed with HFrEF management (ACE, BB, MRA, SGLT2)

Restrictive: MC cause amyloidosis.

HOCM (AD):
- **CCC: systolic anterior motion of the mitral valve**
- Mutation in gene encoding beta-myosin heavy chain OR myosin-binding protein C
- Exam: double apex beat and a harsh systolic murmur
- **Poor prognosis → septal wall thickness >3cm (risk of SCD)**
- Marker for sudden death: abnormal blood pressure on exercise
- Biopsy: myofibrillar hypertrophy, myocyte disarray, interstitial fibrosis
- ECG: deep Q waves in lateral leads, non-specific T wave inversions
- Echo: SAM of anterior mitral valve leaflet with **mitral regurgitation**
- **Left ventricular hypertrophy + outflow tract obstruction**
- **AVOID giving ACE inhibitors (worsens outflow tract obstruction)**
- **Management: Implantable cardioverter defibrillator**
- **MC cause of death: ventricular arrhythmia**

ARVC (arrhythmogenic RV cardiomyopathy):
- **CCC: right ventricular myocardium replaced by fatty and fibro-fatty tissue**
- AD inherited
- ECG: most specific finding is a small positive deflection at the end of the QRS (epsilon wave); T wave inversion V1-V3
- AR variant: Naxos disease (ARVC + woolly hair + palmoplantar keratosis)

**Poor prognostic factors:**
- **S. aureus infection**
- **Prosthetic valve**
- **Negative culture**
- **Low complement levels**

Good prognostic factor: S. viridans.

Organisms:
- S. aureus is the MC cause (esp IV drug users)
- **S. epidermidis: MC cause if <2 months post valve surgery**
- Poor dental hygiene → Viridans (e.g. Sanguinis / Mitis)

**Management — blind therapy:**
- **Native → Amoxicillin (if allergy: Vanco) +/- gentamicin [2x wrong]**
- **Prosthetic → Flucloxacillin (or Vanco) + Rifampicin + low-dose gentamicin [3x wrong]**
- Native valve staphylococci: Flucloxacillin (if allergy: Vanco + Rifampicin)
- Prosthetic valve staphylococci: Flucloxacillin + Rifampicin + low-dose gentamicin
- **Due to Viridans: Benzylpenicillin**

Surgical indications: severe valvular incompetence; aortic abscess (lengthening PR interval); infections resistant to ABx; cardiac failure refractory to treatment; recurrent emboli after ABx.

Modified Duke Criteria: Dx if 2 major // 1 major + 3 minor // 5 minor // pathological. Major: 2 +ve blood cultures, endocardial involvement (+ve echo). Minor: predisposing heart condition, IV drugs, fever >38, major emboli, splenomegaly, clubbing, splinter haemorrhage, Janeway lesions, purpura, glomerulonephritis, Osler's nodes, Roth spots.

Medications:
- **Aspirin + Statin**
- **Sublingual GTN**
- **Beta-blocker** or CCB (BB added first)
- If **CCB** alone: Verapamil or Diltiazem
- If combo: Nifedipine or Amlodipine (also if poor response to max-dose beta-blocker)
- If STILL: add a third drug then PCI

| Drug | MOA | Adverse effects |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Statins | HMG-CoA reductase inhibitors | Myositis, deranged LFTs |
| Fibrates | PPAR-alpha agonist ↑ lipoprotein lipase | Myositis, pruritus, cholestasis |
| Nicotinic acid | Decreases hepatic VLDL secretion | Myositis, flushing |
| Ezetimibe | Decreases cholesterol absorption (small intestine) | Headaches |
| Cholestyramine | Decreases bile acid reabsorption (small intestine) | GI side effects |

- Eruptive xanthomas: familial hypertriglyceridaemia

Arterial supply:
- Left aortic sinus → LCA; Right aortic sinus → RCA
- LCA → LAD + circumflex; RCA → posterior descending
- RCA supplies SA node in 60%, AV node in 90%

Complications:
- Cardiac arrest (**MC, due to V. Fib**)
- Cardiogenic shock
- Chronic heart failure (ventricular ejection fraction is the most important predictor)
- Tachyarrhythmias (VF); bradyarrhythmias (AV block)
- Pericarditis (first 48h); Dressler's syndrome (2-6 weeks)
- LV aneurysm; LV free wall rupture; VSD
- Acute MR (early systolic murmur); rupture of papillary muscle (acute MR: widespread systolic murmur + hypotension + pulmonary oedema)

- RV systolic dysfunction + RV free wall dyskinesia → RV MI (NITRATES AVOIDED due to reducing preload)
- Complete heart block following MI (pallor, bradycardia, hypotension) → right coronary artery affected
- Myoglobin is the first to rise in MI

- Catheter ablation: 2 days off driving
- **Angioplasty (elective): 1 week off driving**
- **Pacemaker insertion: 1 week off driving**
- CABG: 4 weeks off driving
- **ACS: 4 weeks off driving**
- Heart transplant: 6 weeks, no need to notify
- ICD: sustained ventricular arrhythmia → cease driving 6 months + loss of HGV licence; **if prophylactic → 1 month**
- Hypertension: if resting BP >180
- Angina: if symptoms at rest or whilst driving
- Aortic aneurysm ≥6cm: annual review; >6.5cm: permanent ban

- PAH = pulmonary arterial pressure >25
- With **positive response to vasodilator testing [4x wrong]: CCB prescribed**
- **If negative: Sildenafil (phosphodiesterase inhibitor) [2x wrong]**
- **If positive: CCBs (Nifedipine)**
- Decrease in pulmonary arterial pressure upon N.O. inhalation = positive response
- Women with pulmonary hypertension should avoid pregnancy (very high mortality)
- Prostacyclins are the primary treatment; loud second heart sound; diagnosis confirmed with cardiac catheterization; exertional dyspnoea

- Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia: prothrombotic, occurs 5-10 days after starting; Dx serotonin release assay or **antibodies against Heparin PF4 [wrong 3x]** ELISA; treatment: discontinue all heparin and start non-heparin anticoagulation (Argatroban infusion)
- Sotalol (class III): **blocks K channels and has a direct effect on QT interval**
- Adenosine contraindicated in asthma → verapamil given instead
- Phenelzine: high risk of hypertensive crisis
- Diltiazem/CCBs avoided in HF with reduced EF (negative inotropes)
- Pravastatin causes more myopathy than Simvastatin
- Amlodipine: gingival hyperplasia
- Warfarinised patient: check INR 72h before dental procedure, proceed if INR <4
- Statins contraindicated in pregnancy; interact with grapefruit juice
- Verapamil NOT used in V. Tach (precipitates V. Fib); S/E constipation (all CCBs)
- Ivabradine: visual disturbances (phosphenes, green luminescence)
- Beta-blocker use decreased (less likely to prevent stroke, impairs glucose tolerance)
- Warfarin: safe in breastfeeding; target INR for any VTE 2-3 (2.5)
- Erythromycin & Clarithromycin prolong QT; Atropine → mydriasis
- Amiodarone → hypokalaemia; half-life 20-100 days; blocks potassium channels
- ISMN given at asymmetrical dosing to prevent nitrate tolerance
- Moxonidine: central-acting antihypertensive; Hydralazine: direct vasodilator; Diazoxide: vasodilator
- Digoxin toxicity: hypernatraemia, hypokalaemia, hypomagnesaemia, hypothermia, HYPERcalcaemia; shortens QT; reduced eGFR in elderly
- Ticagrelor: dyspnoea after MI (impaired adenosine clearance)
- Nicorandil: substitute for beta-blockers in angina (e.g. patient on verapamil); causes GI tract ulceration
- Flecainide: check for structural heart disease on echo first
- Clopidogrel less effective with Omeprazole
- ACE inhibitors can cause angioedema; Lidocaine is Class Ib
- Thiazides act on the proximal part of the distal convoluted tubule (block Na/Cl symporter); hypokalaemia via **inhibition of Na reabsorption at the beginning of the DCT**; S/E pancreatitis, impotence, agranulocytosis, photosensitivity, hypokalaemia, hyponatraemia, hypercalcaemia, gout, worsen glucose intolerance
- Cranberry juice potentiates warfarin (inhibits CYP2C9)

- Anaphylaxis: 0.5ml 1:1,000 IM
- Cardiac arrest: 10mL 10,000 IV or 1mL of 1:1000 IV // 1mg 1:10,000
- Antidote for epinephrine: phentolamine
- Increases lactate production if infusion

(See the flag on this section — the cardiac-arrest figures read inconsistently in the source and should be verified.)

| Drug | MOA |
| --- | --- |
| Heparin / Fondaparinux | **Activates anti-thrombin III [2x wrong]** |
| Clopidogrel & Ticagrelor | **P2Y12 inhibitor (reversible ADP binding) [3x wrong]** |
| Abciximab | Glycoprotein IIb/IIIa inhibitor |
| Dabigatran / Bivalirudin | Direct thrombin inhibitor |
| Rivaroxaban | Direct factor X inhibitor |
| Aspirin | **Irreversible inhibition of COX 1 and 2 → prevents thromboxane A2 formation in platelets [3x wrong]** |
| Statins | Inhibit HMG-CoA reductase |
| ARBs | Prevent angiotensin II acting on AT1 receptors |
| ACE | Dilation of EFFERENT glomerular arterioles / bradykinin accumulation |
| Class I anti-arrhythmics | Block Nav1.5 Na channels (slow upstroke) |
| Endothelin receptor antagonists (___sentan) | **Decrease pulmonary vascular resistance — used in PAH [2x]** |
| Antiplatelet (Dipyridamole) | **Phosphodiesterase inhibitor, INCREASES effect of adenosine** |
| Thiazides | Inhibit Na/Cl symporter at the DCT |
| Clexane | **Activation of anti-thrombin III → inhibits factor Xa** |
| Sacubitril + ARB | Prevent degradation of BNP and ANP |
| Glycoprotein antagonists (Tirofiban) | **Reversible antagonist of GP IIb/IIIa receptors** |
| Diuretics (Furosemide, _tanide) | Inhibit Na-K-Cl cotransporter in the **ascending limb** of loop of Henle [2x wrong] |
| Amiodarone | Blocks potassium channels (long half-life, loading dose) |
| Flecainide | **Sodium channel blocker (Nav1.5 block)** |
| Digoxin | Na-K ATPase pump inhibitor |
| Amiloride | **Epithelial sodium channel inhibitor at the DCT (K-sparing)** |
| Nicorandil | **Potassium channel activator** |
| Nitrates (isosorbide) | Release NO → activate guanylate cyclase → GTP to cGMP → smooth muscle relaxation |
| Alteplase | Catalyses conversion of plasminogen to plasmin |

- **Hypokalaemia**: peaked T waves, **long QT**; **severe: U waves [2x wrong], ST depression, TWI**
- Sertraline avoided in long QT (antidepressants exacerbate)
- **Hypocalcaemia: long QT**
- **Hypercalcaemia (nausea, abdominal pain, muscle pain/weakness): shortened QT**
- QT measured from start of Q to end of T
- **Long QT + deafness: Jervell and Lange-Nielsen syndrome (AR, mutation in KCNQ1 or KCNE1)**
- J waves: hypothermia; Q waves: previous MI; Delta waves: WPW
- Persistent ST elevation after recent MI with NO chest pain: LV aneurysm
- Torsades de Pointes: polymorphic VT with prolonged QT; associated with TCAs/SAH, hypothermia, Romano-Ward syndrome (MC inherited LQTS); **RF: Clarithromycin (prolongs QT), also Risperidone**; management IV Magnesium Sulfate
- Positive QRS concordance in chest leads → V. Tach

**JVP needs extensive review (multiple times wrong).**
- **Exaggerated V waves (with tricuspid regurgitation)**

A wave → atrial contraction:
- **Cannon: single chamber ventricular pacing**
- **Regular cannon: atrial contractions against closed tricuspid valve (AVNRT)**
- **Irregular cannon: complete heart block OR V. Tach**
- Absent A waves: atrial fibrillation
- Large A waves: pulmonary stenosis / HOCM / tricuspid stenosis

C wave → closure of tricuspid valve (not normally visible).
V wave → passive filling against **a closed tricuspid valve**:
- **Giant: decompensated right heart failure / tricuspid regurgitation**
- Absent V waves: severe hypovolaemia or complete heart block

**X wave → fall in atrial pressure during ventricular systole [4x wrong]**:
- Exaggerated X descent: constrictive pericarditis and cardiac tamponade
- NO Y descent is characteristic of tamponade

**Y wave → opening of tricuspid valve**:
- Slow Y descent: tricuspid stenosis
- Exaggerated Y descent: constrictive pericarditis

AV dissociation (P waves dissociated from QRS): **complete heart block — variable intensity of S1**; **also V. Tach**

| Left axis deviation | Right axis deviation |
| --- | --- |
| Ostium primum ASD | Ostium secundum ASD |
| Left posterior hemiblock | Obesity |
| Left BBB | Right ventricular hypertrophy |
| Left anterior hemiblock | Pulmonary embolism |
| WPW + right-sided accessory pathway | Chronic lung disease |

- **If bio-prosthetic: must be on aspirin [3x wrong]**
- **If mechanical: must be on warfarin and aspirin**
- **Lower failure rate than bio-prosthetic**
- Warfarin: lifelong
- **Aortic valve: INR target 3.0**
- **Mitral valve: INR target 3.5 [2x wrong]**

Second heart sound:
- **Loud: Pulmonary HTN [3x wrong]**
- Soft: AS
- Fixed split: ASD
- **Reversed split (occurs before S1): LBBB**

Third heart sound (S3): constrictive pericarditis; LV failure (dilated cardiomyopathy) & MR; caused by diastolic filling of the ventricle; normal if <30 (or <50 in women). **Gallop rhythm (S3) is a sign of early LVF.**

Pulses:
- **Alternans**: seen in HF (systolic dysfunction) — upstroke alternates strong/weak
- Collapsing: forceful rapid upstroke and descent
- Jerky: rapid forceful upstroke
- Slow: slow upstroke
- **Bisferiens: 2 sharp upstrokes during systole**
- **Mixed aortic valve disease is most commonly associated with bisferiens pulse (2 systolic peaks + normal dicrotic notch) [2x wrong]**

- INR >8 with no bleeding: Oral Vit K + stop warfarin + repeat in 24h
- INR >9 with bleeding: Oral Vit K + stop warfarin + repeat in 24h
- Warfarin restarted when INR is below 5
- Any major bleeding: stop warfarin, give Vit K & PCC
- Emergency surgery on warfarin: give four-factor prothrombin complex concentrate; if surgery can wait 6-8h: 5mg Vitamin K IV
- Recurrent DVT: INR goal of 3.5
- Obesity gives a false -ve for heart failure

Aortic dissection:
- Associated with bicuspid aortic valve, Turner, Noonan, pregnancy, Marfan
- Vague abdominal pain
- Type A (dissection flap proximal to the brachiocephalic vessels): ascending aorta — control BP + surgery
- Type B: descending aorta — control BP

Carotid artery dissection: young, anterior circulation stroke (hemiparesis), head/neck pain after minor trauma; classic ipsilateral partial Horner syndrome (ptosis and miosis); initial management anti-thrombotic (aspirin).

- Post-ductal (upper limb BP > lower limb): aortic coarctation — associated with neurofibromatosis, bicuspid aortic valve, Turner's, berry aneurysms
- Takayasu arteritis: young Asian woman, headache, malaise, joint pain, claudication, significant BP difference between arms; obliterative arteritis affecting **the aorta**; associated with AR
- AKI following angiography: cholesterol emboli (high urea, high eosinophils); livedo reticularis
- Blue toe syndrome: cholesterol embolisation

- Primary cardiac tumours MC site: left atrium (atrial myxoma). Myxomas: clubbing, mid-diastolic murmur, pyrexia, AF
- Multifocal atrial tachycardia: Verapamil
- Pre-eclampsia: restrict fluids (pulmonary/cerebral oedema); low-dose aspirin in next pregnancy if previous; ACLS beyond 20 weeks — left lateral tilt of 15 degrees
- PE duration: unprovoked continue 6 months; provoked 3 months; massive PE + hypotension → thrombolyse
- Troponin: component of thin filaments, binds tropomyosin, controls contraction/relaxation
- Electrical cardioversion synchronised to the R wave
- SVT not relieved by vagal/adenosine → verapamil (likely AVNRT)
- Migraine + stroke = patent foramen ovale (MC cause of paradoxical embolus)
- Ebstein's anomaly: tricuspid regurgitation **(pan[holo]systolic murmur worse on inspiration) [3x wrong]**; **arrhythmia and SOB on exertion**; associated with lithium in pregnancy; ECG RBBB and WPW; giant V waves on JVP
- WPW: associated with MVP, Ebstein's, thyrotoxicosis, HOCM; **wide QRS with a slurred upstroke**; **Verapamil contraindicated/dangerous** (precipitates VF); meds Sotalol, Amiodarone, **Flecainide**; definitive treatment radiofrequency ablation of accessory pathway
- **Colorectal Ca associated with Streptococcus Bovis (Gallolyticus)**
- Catecholaminergic polymorphic VT: symptoms before 20; normal resting ECG (bidirectional VT on exercise); beta-blockers first line; calcium channel defects
- **Brugada syndrome: mutation in the myocardial sodium ion channel proteins**; febrile, young, southeast Asian, or presents with fainting; ECG convex **ST elevation V1-V3** (type 1); **SCN5A gene (alpha subunit)**
- Cardiac syndrome X: angina features, minimal atherosclerosis, ECG downsloping ST depression
- Atrial flutter: permanent management radiofrequency ablation of the tricuspid valve isthmus; atrial 300/min + ventricular 150/min
- Eisenmenger syndrome: left-to-right shunt due to pulmonary hypertension; VSD/ASD/PDA; cyanosis, raised JVP, loud S2, large A waves
- BNP: **suppresses aldosterone secretion [2x wrong]**; secreted by ventricles; diuretic; rises in LV failure; reduces sympathetic tone; vasodilator
- ICD indications: previous MI with non-sustained VT on 24h monitoring; HOCM; previous cardiac arrest due to VF; long QT syndrome
- Temporary pacemaker: symptomatic bradycardia not responding to atropine; post anterior MI; trifascicular block prior to surgery
- Imaging: Technetium-99m most common radionuclide (SPECT); Thallium — myocardial perfusion; FDG — PET; palpitations investigated with 72h Holter monitors; MUGA scan most accurate for LV function; pulmonary capillary wedge pressure via Swan-Ganz (estimates LA pressure)
- Histology: Councilman bodies (Hep C & yellow fever); Mallory bodies (alcoholism); Call-Exner bodies (granulosa cell tumour); Schiller-Duval bodies (yolk sac tumour)
- During exercise: venous constriction; 3-fold increase in HR; 50% increase in stroke volume; increased contractility; stable/slight decrease in DBP
- Stop a TMT if: BP 105/70 or less, or 250 systolic / 115 diastolic; HR 220 minus age
- Drug-eluting stent: longer duration of clopidogrel

cards.q: Aortic stenosis: the pulse and S2 features (repeatedly wrong)?
cards.a: **Slow rising [4x wrong]** pulse, narrow pulse pressure, delayed ESM, **soft S2**. Severe: soft S2, displaced apex, **S4 (before S1) [3x wrong]** — **S4 coincides with the P waves on ECG [2x wrong]**.

cards.q: Aortic stenosis: indications for valve replacement?
cards.a: **Symptomatic (exertional dyspnoea)**, **peak gradient >40**, or **valve area <1**. MC cause: bicuspid valve if <65, calcification if >65.

cards.q: Aortic regurgitation: the murmur (repeatedly wrong)?
cards.a: **Early diastolic murmur [4x wrong].** Associated with rheumatic fever, syphilis, Marfan's, ankylosing spondylitis, bicuspid valve.

cards.q: Mitral stenosis: murmur and severe management (wrong before)?
cards.a: **Mid diastolic rumbling murmur** at the apex; severity = longer murmur. Severe (rheumatic): **Percutaneous mitral commissurotomy [2x wrong].**

cards.q: Tricuspid regurgitation: two repeatedly-wrong features?
cards.a: **Systolic murmur louder during inspiration [2x wrong]** and **left parasternal heave [3x wrong].**

cards.q: Congenital heart disease: commonest cyanotic lesion at birth vs overall?
cards.a: **TGA is MC at birth**, but **Fallot's is most common overall.** Acyanotic: VSD most common.

cards.q: Acute pericarditis: ECG changes and first-line management?
cards.a: Widespread saddle-shaped ST elevation with **PR depression**. NSAIDs + Colchicine for 1-2 weeks; inpatient only if high fever or raised troponin.

cards.q: AF: CHA2DS2-VASc anticoagulation thresholds, and duration for a single episode?
cards.a: Score 0 none; 1 consider (males); ≥2 offer DOAC. **4 weeks warfarin and review → 1 episode**; lifelong if 2+ episodes. Idarucizumab reverses dabigatran.

cards.q: HOCM: the classic echo finding and the drug to AVOID (repeatedly wrong)?
cards.a: **Systolic anterior motion (SAM) of the mitral valve**; **LVH + outflow tract obstruction**. **AVOID ACE inhibitors (worsen outflow obstruction).**

cards.q: HOCM: poor prognosis marker, management, and commonest cause of death?
cards.a: **Septal wall thickness >3cm (SCD risk)**; **management ICD**; **MC cause of death: ventricular arrhythmia.** AD; beta-myosin heavy chain / myosin-binding protein C.

cards.q: ARVC: characteristic pathology?
cards.a: **RV myocardium replaced by fatty and fibro-fatty tissue.** AD; epsilon wave (small positive deflection at end of QRS); TWI V1-V3; Naxos = ARVC + woolly hair + palmoplantar keratosis.

cards.q: Infective endocarditis: poor prognostic factors?
cards.a: **S. aureus, prosthetic valve, negative culture, low complement.** Good: S. viridans. S. aureus MC overall; **S. epidermidis MC if <2 months post valve surgery.**

cards.q: IE blind therapy: native vs prosthetic valve (repeatedly wrong)?
cards.a: **Native → Amoxicillin (Vanco if allergy) +/- gentamicin [2x wrong].** **Prosthetic → Flucloxacillin (or Vanco) + Rifampicin + low-dose gentamicin [3x wrong].** Viridans → **Benzylpenicillin.**

cards.q: Stable angina: medication ladder?
cards.a: **Aspirin + Statin**, **sublingual GTN**, **beta-blocker** (or CCB — BB first). CCB alone: Verapamil/Diltiazem. Combo: Nifedipine/Amlodipine. Then third drug, then PCI.

cards.q: MI: commonest cause of cardiac arrest, and which coronary in complete heart block post-MI?
cards.a: Cardiac arrest is the **MC complication, due to V. Fib**. Complete heart block post-MI (pallor, bradycardia, hypotension) → right coronary artery. RV MI → avoid nitrates.

cards.q: DVLA: off-driving times for angioplasty, pacemaker, and ACS (repeatedly wrong)?
cards.a: **Elective angioplasty: 1 week. Pacemaker: 1 week. ACS: 4 weeks.** Catheter ablation 2 days; CABG 4 weeks; prophylactic ICD 1 month.

cards.q: Pulmonary arterial hypertension: treatment by vasodilator response (repeatedly wrong)?
cards.a: **Positive response to vasodilator testing [4x wrong] → CCB (Nifedipine).** **Negative → Sildenafil (PDE inhibitor) [2x wrong].** Positive response = fall in pressure on N.O. inhalation.

cards.q: Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia: diagnosis antibody and treatment?
cards.a: **Antibodies against Heparin-PF4 [3x wrong]** (ELISA) or serotonin release assay; prothrombotic, 5-10 days after starting; stop all heparin, start Argatroban.

cards.q: Antiplatelet MOA: heparin, clopidogrel/ticagrelor, aspirin (repeatedly wrong)?
cards.a: Heparin/Fondaparinux **activate anti-thrombin III [2x wrong]**; Clopidogrel/Ticagrelor **P2Y12 inhibitor [3x wrong]**; Aspirin **irreversible COX 1&2 → prevents thromboxane A2 [3x wrong].**

cards.q: MOA: Nicorandil, Amiloride, Dipyridamole, Tirofiban?
cards.a: Nicorandil = **K channel activator**; Amiloride = **ENaC inhibitor (K-sparing)**; Dipyridamole = **PDE inhibitor, increases adenosine**; Tirofiban = **reversible GP IIb/IIIa antagonist**.

cards.q: Furosemide MOA and site (wrong before)?
cards.a: Inhibits the **Na-K-Cl cotransporter in the ascending limb of the loop of Henle [2x wrong].**

cards.q: ECG in hypokalaemia (repeatedly wrong)?
cards.a: Peaked T waves, **long QT**; severe: **U waves [2x wrong], ST depression, TWI.** Sertraline avoided in long QT.

cards.q: QT changes: hypocalcaemia vs hypercalcaemia?
cards.a: **Hypocalcaemia: long QT. Hypercalcaemia: short QT.** Long QT + deafness = **Jervell and Lange-Nielsen (AR, KCNQ1/KCNE1).**

cards.q: JVP: cannon A waves and the X wave (repeatedly wrong)?
cards.a: **Cannon = single chamber ventricular pacing; regular = AVNRT; irregular = complete heart block or V. Tach.** **X wave = fall in atrial pressure during ventricular systole [4x wrong].** Exaggerated X: constrictive pericarditis/tamponade.

cards.q: JVP: exaggerated V waves and complete heart block sign?
cards.a: **Exaggerated V waves = tricuspid regurgitation** (also decompensated right HF). Complete heart block → **variable intensity of S1** (AV dissociation); also seen in V. Tach.

cards.q: Torsades de Pointes: two drug risk factors and management?
cards.a: **Clarithromycin (prolongs QT) and Risperidone.** Polymorphic VT with prolonged QT; management IV Magnesium Sulfate. Romano-Ward = MC inherited LQTS.

cards.q: Prosthetic valves: antithrombotic and INR targets (repeatedly wrong)?
cards.a: **Bio-prosthetic → aspirin [3x wrong].** **Mechanical → warfarin + aspirin** (lifelong; lower failure rate). **Aortic INR 3.0; Mitral INR 3.5 [2x wrong].**

cards.q: Second heart sound: loud vs reversed split (repeatedly wrong)?
cards.a: **Loud S2: Pulmonary HTN [3x wrong].** Soft: AS. Fixed split: ASD. **Reversed split (before S1): LBBB.**

cards.q: Bisferiens pulse — what is it and its association (wrong before)?
cards.a: **Two sharp upstrokes during systole.** **Mixed aortic valve disease is most commonly associated with bisferiens pulse [2x wrong]** (2 systolic peaks + normal dicrotic notch). Pulsus alternans = HF.

cards.q: Ebstein's anomaly: murmur and features (repeatedly wrong)?
cards.a: Tricuspid regurgitation — **pan(holo)systolic murmur worse on inspiration [3x wrong]**; **arrhythmia and SOB on exertion**; lithium in pregnancy; RBBB + WPW; giant V waves.

cards.q: WPW: ECG, contraindicated drug, and definitive treatment?
cards.a: **Wide QRS with slurred upstroke (delta wave)**; **Verapamil contraindicated (precipitates VF)**; meds Sotalol/Amiodarone/**Flecainide**; definitive = radiofrequency ablation of the accessory pathway.

cards.q: Brugada syndrome: gene, channel and ECG?
cards.a: Mutation in **myocardial sodium ion channel** — **SCN5A (alpha subunit)**; **convex ST elevation V1-V3** (type 1); young SE Asian, febrile, or fainting.

cards.q: BNP actions (wrong before)?
cards.a: **Suppresses aldosterone secretion [2x wrong]**; secreted by ventricles; diuretic; vasodilator; reduces sympathetic tone; rises in LV failure.

cards.q: Endocarditis organism linked to colorectal cancer, and to poor dental hygiene?
cards.a: **Colorectal Ca → Streptococcus bovis (gallolyticus).** Poor dental hygiene → **Viridans (Sanguinis).**

cards.q: Gallop rhythm (S3) — significance?
cards.a: **A sign of early LVF.** S3 also in constrictive pericarditis and MR; normal if <30 (or <50 in women).

cards.q: Dilated cardiomyopathy: causes and management?
cards.a: MC idiopathic; also coxsackie, HTN, haemochromatosis, alcohol, DMD, thiamine deficiency, selenium (Keshan). Balloon heart on CXR. Managed as HFrEF (ACE, BB, MRA, SGLT2).

cards.q: Modified Duke criteria: how is IE diagnosed?
cards.a: 2 major, or 1 major + 3 minor, or 5 minor, or pathological. Major: 2 +ve blood cultures, endocardial involvement (+ve echo). Aortic abscess suggested by lengthening PR interval.

cards.q: MI arterial supply: SA and AV node?
cards.a: RCA supplies the SA node in 60% and the AV node in 90%. LCA → LAD + circumflex; RCA → posterior descending.

cards.q: Aortic dissection: Type A vs Type B management?
cards.a: Type A (proximal to brachiocephalic vessels, ascending aorta): control BP + surgery. Type B (descending): control BP. Associated with bicuspid valve, Turner, Noonan, pregnancy, Marfan.

cards.q: Warfarin: INR >8 no bleeding vs major bleeding management?
cards.a: INR >8 no bleed: oral Vit K + stop warfarin, repeat 24h; restart when INR <5. Major bleeding: stop warfarin, give Vit K + PCC. Emergency surgery: four-factor PCC.

cards.q: Eisenmenger syndrome: mechanism and features?
cards.a: Left-to-right shunt reversing due to pulmonary hypertension (VSD/ASD/PDA). Cyanosis, raised JVP, loud S2, large A waves.

cards.q: Cardiac imaging: which radionuclide/scan for what?
cards.a: Technetium-99m = most common (SPECT); Thallium = myocardial perfusion; FDG = PET; MUGA = most accurate LV function; palpitations = 72h Holter; PCWP via Swan-Ganz estimates LA pressure.

cards.q: AKI after angiography — cause and signs?
cards.a: Cholesterol emboli — high urea, high eosinophils, livedo reticularis. Ventricular arrhythmia post-angiogram is usually transient and safe to discharge.

cards.q: Takayasu arteritis: presentation?
cards.a: Young Asian woman: headache, malaise, joint pain, claudication, significant BP difference between arms. Obliterative arteritis of the aorta; associated with AR.
